The empire's militarism and alliance system contributed directly to World War I, which killed approximately 17 million people. ETHICS-003: The empire was proclaimed in Versailles (1871), a deliberately humiliating location for France, illustrating the nationalist aggression embedded in the state's founding.

name_variants.name: Zweites Reich
name_variants.context: German informal denomination meaning 'Second Empire' (after the Holy Roman Empire)

territory_changes.description: Treaty of Frankfurt: France ceded Alsace and the Moselle department of Lorraine to the new German Empire following the Franco-Prussian War. The population (approximately 1.5 million) was not consulted. Those who wished to remain French were given until 1872 to emigrate. The annexation created deep Franco-German enmity that contributed to both World Wars.

territory_changes.region: German South-West Africa (Namibia), Kamerun, Togoland, German East Africa
territory_changes.description: Germany rapidly acquired colonial territories in Africa. German colonial rule was characterized by extreme violence, including the genocide of the Herero and Nama peoples in South-West Africa (1904-1908). General Lothar von Trotha's extermination order (Vernichtungsbefehl) explicitly called for the annihilation of the Herero people. An estimated 65,000-80,000 Herero (80% of the population) and 10,000 Nama (50% of the population) were killed through warfare, concentration camps, and forced desert marches.

Military defeat in World War I led to the German Revolution, Kaiser Wilhelm II's abdication, and the proclamation of the Weimar Republic. The Treaty of Versailles (1919) stripped Germany of its colonies, Alsace-Lorraine, and significant eastern territories. The empire's collapse and the perceived humiliation of Versailles created conditions exploited by the Nazi movement.

sources.citation: Nipperdey, T. Deutsche Geschichte 1866-1918 (1990-1992)
Verfassung des Deutschen Reiches (Constitution of the German Empire, 1871)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in German. The Hungarian name 'Osztrak-Magyar Monarchia' is equally valid as the state was officially bilingual at the imperial level. ETHICS-003: The dual monarchy had two capitals (Vienna and Budapest), two parliaments, and two governments, making it uniquely complex. The entry lists Vienna as the primary capital as seat of the emperor and shared foreign ministry. ETHICS-002: While the Compromise of 1867 granted Hungary autonomy, it left the Slavic, Romanian, and other minorities of both halves without comparable political representation. Hungarian Magyarization and Austrian Germanization policies suppressed minority languages and cultures. The empire's annexation of Bosnia-Herzegovina (1908) contributed to the tensions that triggered World War I, which killed approximately 17 million people.

territory_changes.description: Congress of Berlin authorized Austria-Hungary to occupy and administer Bosnia-Herzegovina (nominally still Ottoman). The occupation met significant armed resistance from Bosnian Muslims and Orthodox Serbs. The formal annexation in 1908 created an international crisis. Bosnian resentment of Austro-Hungarian rule contributed directly to the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in 1914, triggering World War I.

Military defeat in World War I led to the dissolution of Austria-Hungary into multiple successor states: Austria, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, and the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes (Yugoslavia), with territories also going to Romania, Poland, and Italy. The Treaties of Saint-Germain (1919) and Trianon (1920) formalized the partition. Approximately 3 million ethnic Hungarians found themselves outside Hungary's new borders, creating lasting irredentist tensions.

sources.citation: Judson, P. The Habsburg Empire: A New History (2016)
Ausgleich (Austro-Hungarian Compromise, 1867)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: name_original in Italian. ETHICS-002: Italian unification (Risorgimento) is often romanticized but involved the military conquest of the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies, which experienced the Risorgimento as a northern invasion. The subsequent suppression of southern Italian resistance ('brigandage') involved summary executions, village destruction, and the internment of thousands. Italy's colonial empire involved atrocities in Libya (1911-1943, including concentration camps and chemical weapons), Ethiopia (1935-1941, use of mustard gas, massacre of Addis Ababa), Eritrea, and Somalia. The fascist period (1922-1943) saw the empire at its most brutal. ETHICS-003: Rome was the capital from 1871; before that Turin (1861-1865) and Florence (1865-1871) served as capital.

name_variants.name: Regnum Italiae
name_variants.context: Latin form used in formal and ecclesiastical contexts

territory_changes.description: Proclamation of the Kingdom of Italy under Victor Emmanuel II of Sardinia-Piedmont. The unification incorporated the Kingdom of the Two Sicilies (conquered by Garibaldi's expedition), the Papal States (partly), and the former Austrian territories. Southern Italy experienced the 'Risorgimento' largely as a northern military conquest, and subsequent resistance ('brigandage') was suppressed with extreme violence, including estimated 100,000 southern Italian casualties.

Fascist Italy invaded Ethiopia in violation of international law. Italian forces used mustard gas, bombed Red Cross hospitals, and massacred civilians. The Addis Ababa massacre (February 1937) following an assassination attempt on Marshal Graziani killed an estimated 19,000-30,000 Ethiopians. Italy's occupation was characterized by racist colonial policies and systematic atrocities. These crimes have been insufficiently acknowledged in Italian public memory.

Constitutional referendum abolished the monarchy (54.3% voted for a republic). King Umberto II went into exile. The Italian Republic was proclaimed on June 2, 1946. Italy also lost its colonies and the territories of Istria and Dalmatia (to Yugoslavia), resulting in the forced displacement of approximately 250,000-350,000 Italian-speaking 'esuli' (Julian-Dalmatian exodus).

sources.citation: Duggan, C. The Force of Destiny: A History of Italy Since 1796 (2007)
Statuto Albertino (1848, constitutional basis of the Kingdom)
